revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تراث شعبي ياباني]][[ca:Folklore japonès]][[en:Japanese folklore]][[eo:Japana folkloro]][[es:Folclore de Japón]][[fa:فولکلور ژاپنی]][[id:Folklor Jepang]][[it:Folclore giapponese]][[ja:日本の民間伝承]][[pl:Folklor japoński]][[pt:Folclore japonês]][[ru:Японский фольклор]][[simple:Japanese folklore]][[sq:Folklori japonez]][[tcy:ಜಪಾನ್‍ ಜಾನಪದ ಕಲೆ]][[tl:Tradisyong-pambayang Hapones]][[tr:Japon folklorü]][[uk:Японський фольклор]][[uz:Yapon folklori]][[vi:Văn hóa dân gian Nhật Bản]]
{{short description|Folk traditions of Japan}} {{Multiple issues| {{More footnotes needed|date=May 2012}} {{Cleanup|reason=Links to the Japanese Wikipedia are not properly formatted|date=December 2014}} }} {{Jmyth infobox}} '''Japanese folklore''' encompasses the informally learned [[:en:folk tradition|folk tradition]]s of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and the {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese people|1=Japanese people}} as expressed in its [[:en:oral traditions|oral traditions]], {{ill|Tradition|en|Tradition|qid=Q82821|lt=customs|1=Tradition}}, and {{ill|Material culture|en|Material culture|qid=Q2143054|lt=Material culture|1=Material culture}}.
In Japanese, the term {{Nihongo|''minkan denshō''|民間伝承||"transmissions among the folk"}} is used to describe {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|qid=Q36192|lt=Folklore|1=Folklore}}. The {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|qid=Q132116|lt=academic study of folklore|1=Folklore studies}} is known as {{Nihongo|''minzokugaku''|民俗学}}. Folklorists also employ the term {{Nihongo|''minzoku shiryō''|民俗資料}} or {{Nihongo|"folklore material"|民俗資料}} to refer to the objects and arts they study.
==Folk religion== {{Main|Shinto}} Men dressed as {{ill|Namahage|en|Namahage|qid=Q1133889|lt=Namahage|1=Namahage}}, wearing ogre-like masks and traditional straw capes (''{{ill|Mino (straw cape)|en|Mino (straw cape)|qid=Q708965|lt=mino|1=Mino (straw cape)}}'') make rounds of homes,<ref>{{Harvnb|Bownas|Brown|2004}}, p.50-2 misidentifies Namahage as a Kyushu ritual. See other sources under {{ill|Namahage|en|Namahage|qid=Q1133889|lt=Namahage|1=Namahage}} article</ref> in an annual ritual of the {{ill|Oga Peninsula|en|Oga Peninsula|qid=Q1132109|lt=Oga Peninsula|1=Oga Peninsula}} area of the Northeast region. These ogre-men masquerade as [[kami]] looking to instill fear in the children who are lazily idling around the fire. This is a particularly colorful example of folk practice still kept alive.
A parallel custom is the secretive {{ill|Akamata-Kuromata|ja|3=アカマタ・クロマタ|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akamata-Kuromata|1=Akamata and Kuromata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11282723}} ritual of the {{ill|Yaeyama Islands|en|Yaeyama Islands|qid=Q696496|lt=Yaeyama Islands|1=Yaeyama Islands}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} which does not allow itself to be photographed.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ayabe |first=Tsuneo|editor-first=Agrhananda |editor-last=Bharati |chapter=Esoteric Rituals in Japanese Traditional Secret Societeis: A Study of the Death and Rebirth Motif |title=Agents and Audiences|publisher=Walter de Gruyter |year=1976 |isbn=978-3-dd-080584-0 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-Rhy_9p9BZQC&pg=PA385}}</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|Plutschow|1990}}, p.60 misplaces as [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima prefecture]], probably confusing it with {{ill|Toshidon|ja|3=トシドン|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshidon|1=Toshidon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2255821}} of the [[:en:Koshikijima Islands|Koshikijima Islands]] which is mentioned by {{Harvnb|Bocking|1997}}, p.87 (marebito), p.98 (namahage)</ref>
Many, though increasingly fewer households maintain a [[kamidana]] or a small [[Shinto]] altar shelf.<ref>{{Harvnb|Bestor|Bestor|20dd|p=69}}, households with kamidana showed a decline from 62% (1984) to 43.9% (2006); and only 26.4% in metropolitan areas</ref> The Shinto version of the {{ill|Kitchen god|en|Kitchen god|qid=Q105619939|lt=Kitchen god|1=Kitchen god}} is the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kamado|en|Kamado|qid=Q12177411|lt=Kamado|1=Kamado}} kami|[[:ja:かまど神|かまど神]]}}, and the syncretic Buddhist version is the {{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|qid=Q6455190|lt=Kōjin|1=Kōjin}}, a deity of the hearth enshrined in the kitchen.
Japanese popular cults or {{Nihongo|''kō''|[[:ja:講|講]]}}<ref>{{cite book|editor-last=Heibonsha|script-title=ja:世界百科事典 |title=Sekai hyakka jiten|year=1964|volume=21|page=442 |chapter=minkan shinkō|script-chapter=ja:民間信仰 |trans-chapter=folk religion |last=Takeda |first=Chōshū}} Mentions such kō as those devoted to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]](伊勢講)、Akiba(秋葉講)、Ōmine(大峰講)、[[kōshin]](庚申講)、Koyasu(子安講)、{{ill|Mountain God|en|Mountain God|qid=Q16256547|lt=Mountain God|1=Mountain God}} (山ノ神講)、{{ill|Nenbutsu kō|ja|3=念仏講|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenbutsu kō|1=nenbutsukou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11491436}}(念仏講), [[Kannon]] (観音講)</ref> are sometimes devoted to particular deities and buddhas, e.g. the angry [[Acala]] or the healer [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]]. But many cults centered around paying respects to sacred sites such as the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]] (''Ise-kō'' or ''{{ill|okage-mairi|ja|3=お蔭参り|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11263449}}'') or {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} (''{{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|qid=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-kō|1=Fujiko (religion)}}'', by which many local mock-Fuji shrines have been erected). Pilgrimage to these meccas declined after the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. But recently, the {{ill|Shikoku Pilgrimage|en|Shikoku Pilgrimage|qid=Q1972399|lt=Shikoku Pilgrimage|1=Shikoku Pilgrimage}} of the eighty-eight temple sites (commonly known as ''ohenro-san'') has become fashionable. Popular media and cottage industries now extoll a number of shrines and sacred natural sites as {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|qid=Q11329568|lt=power spots|1=Power spot (spirituality)}}.
There is a long list of practices performed to ward evil ({{Nihongo|yakuyoke|厄除け|}})<ref name=schnell>{{Harvnb|Schnelle|1999}}, p.325, note 23 "the ''okoshi daiko'' as a "ceremony to guard against misfortune" ("''yakuyoke no gyōji''")"</ref> or expel evil ({{Nihongo|yakubarai, oharai|{{ill|Apotropaic magic|en|Apotropaic magic|qid=Q620591|lt=yaku-barai|1=Apotropaic magic}}}}), e.g. sounding the drums.<ref name=schnell/> In some areas it is common to place a small mound of salt outside the house (''{{ill|morijio|ja|3=盛り塩|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morijio|1=Q11581314|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581314}}'').<ref name=bownas023>{{Harvnb|Bownas|Brown|2004}}, p.23, "Salt, the sophistication of ritual sea bathing as a cleanser of contamination, appears today even in many apparently secular uses. The sumō wrestler will sprinkle [salt] across the ring as he advances.. a restaurant frequently has its Fuji-cone of caked salt by the door-jamb, as a means of clearing the defilement left by an unwelcome patron".</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Hosking|first=Richard|title=A Dictionary of Japanese Food: Ingredients & Culture|publisher= Tuttle Publishing |year=1997 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1avA7zEYCQ0C&pg=PA98 |isbn=978-0-8048-2042-4}}, p.98, "little piles of salt have been placed at shrines to purify and gain the gods' protective presence"</ref> Salt-scattering is generally considered purifying<ref name=bownas023/> (it is employed in {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}} tournaments,<ref name=bownas023/> to give a well-known example). A stock routine in period or even contemporary drama involves a master of the house telling his wife to scatter salt after an undesirable visitor has just left. Contrarily, lighting sparks with {{ill|flint|en|flint|qid=Q83087|lt=flint|1=Flint}} just as a someone is leaving the house was considered lucky.
No one now engages in the silent vigil required by the ''[[Kōshin]]'' cult, but it might be noted that this cult has been associated with the iconic three [[:en:See no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil|See no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil]] monkeys.<ref name="Murakami 1988">{{Harvnb|Murakami|1988}}, p.53</ref>
There are certain vestiges of {{ill|Geomancy|en|Geomancy|qid=Q815750|lt=Geomancy|1=Geomancy}} introduced into Japan from China through [[Onmyōdō]]. The word ''{{ill|kimon (ogre gate)|ja|3=鬼門|lt=kimon|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kimon (ogre gate)|1=Kimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673539}}'', "ogre's gate", colloquially refers to anything that a person may have constant ill luck with, but in the original sense designates the northeasterly direction, considered to be unlucky or dangerously inviting of ill-intended spirits<ref name="Murakami 1988"/> (cf. {{ill|Konjin|en|Konjin|qid=Q6429460|lt=Konjin|1=Konjin}}). There is also a Japanese version of [[:en:Feng Shui|Feng Shui]] known as ''{{ill|kasō (house physiognomy)|ja|3=家相|lt=kasō|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kasō (house physiognomy)|1=kasō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455554}}''<ref>{{cite book|last1= Jeremy|last2= Robinson|first1=Michael Ernes |title=Ceremony and Symbolism in the Japanese Home |publisher=Manchester University Press ND|year=1989 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SQXCAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA125 |isbn=978-0-7190-2506-8}}, pp.125- goes into a description of kasō in considerable detail.</ref> or literally "house physiognomy". Closely connected is the {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|qid=Q62744|lt=Yin-yang|1=Yin and yang}} path or [[Onmyōdō]], and its concepts such as ''{{ill|katatagae|"direction changing"|3=方違え|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/katatagae|qq=}}'' also known as ''kataimi'',<ref>{{Harvnb|Kornicki|McMullen|1996|p=87}}; citing {{Harvnb|Bernard|1958}}</ref> which was widely practiced by nobles in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. A widely known taboo (''{{ill|kitamakura|ja|3=北枕|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kitamakura|1=kita makura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11402230}}'') advises against sleeping with your head faced north,<ref name=lock>{{cite book |last=Lock|first=Margaret M.|title=East Asian Medicine in Urban Japan: Varieties of Medical Experience|publisher=University of California Press|year=1984 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pxx6UaxDNZAC&pg=PA98|isbn= 978-0-520-05231-4}}, p.98 her informants do not believe in it, but rather not be seen</ref> though it is doubtful if anyone now seriously heeds this prohibition.<ref name=lock/>
In Japanese folklore, [[:en:pheasants|pheasants]] were considered messengers from heaven. However, researchers from Japan's Graduate University for Advanced Studies and National Institute of Polar Research claimed in March 2020 that red pheasant tails witnessed across the night sky over Japan in 620 A.D., might be a red {{ill|aurora|en|aurora|qid=Q40609|lt=aurora|1=Aurora}} produced during a magnetic storm.<ref name="phys.org">{{cite web|url=https://phys.org/news/2020-03-modern-science-reveals-ancient-secret.html|title=Modern science reveals ancient secret in Japanese literature|website=phys.org|date=30 March 2020|access-date=3 April 2020|archive-date=1 April 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200401084459/https://phys.org/news/2020-03-modern-science-reveals-ancient-secret.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Folktales== {{See also|Japanese folktales}} [[File:Hokusai tanuki tea kettle.jpg|thumb|upright=0.8|A raccoon dog half transformed into a cauldron hangs from a ''jizai kagi'' hook over an {{ill|Irori|en|Irori|qid=Q1185514|lt=Irori|1=Irori}} hearth (scene from the tale ''{{ill|Bunbuku Chagama|en|Bunbuku Chagama|qid=Q1367705|lt=Bunbuku Chagama|1=Bunbuku Chagama}}''). C. 1840s, School of {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|qid=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}.]]
As in most developed nations, it is increasingly difficult to find living storytellers of oral tradition. But there is a wealth of folktales collected through the ages. The name ''mukashi-banashi'' (tales of "long ago" or from "bygone times") has been applied to the common folktale, since they typically open with the formula "Mukashi..."<ref name=heibonsha-mukashi>{{cite book|editor-last=Heibonsha|script-title=ja:世界百科事典 |title=Sekai hyakka jiten |year=1964|volume=21|pages=499–502 |chapter=mukashibanashi|script-chapter=ja:昔話 |trans-chapter=Japanese tales section|first=Katsumi |last=Masuda}}</ref> (akin to "Once upon a time..."). They also close with some set phrase like "''dotto harai''"<ref name=heibonsha-mukashi/> (a variant form being {{ill|Dondo Hare|en|Dondo Hare|qid=Q4184202|lt=Dondo Hare|1=Dondo Hare}}).
These tales had been told in their local dialects, which may be difficult to understand to outsiders, both because of intonation and pronunciation differences, conjugations, and vocabulary. Many folktales collected from the field are actually "translations" into standard Japanese (or more like adaptations, merging several collected versions).
===Classic folktales=== Classic folktales such as ''[[Momotarō]]'', which most Japanese today are familiarized through pictured children's storybooks, manga, or other popularizations, can be traced to {{ill|E-hon|en|E-hon|qid=Q3045706|lt=picture-books|1=E-hon}} printed in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], though their prototypical stories may go back much further. The versions retold by children's story author {{ill|Sazanami Iwaya|ja|3=巌谷小波|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sazanami Iwaya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巖谷小波|1=Sazanami Iwaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q19366455}} (1870–1933; often considered the {{ill|Charles Perrault|en|Charles Perrault|qid=Q128460|lt=Perrault|1=Charles Perrault}} of Japan)<ref>{{cite book|last=Kinoshita |first=Junji |title=Hyōronshu (collected criticisms 1956~1957) |volume=4|publisher=Miraisha (未来社) |year=1974 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ubk1AQAAIAAJ |page=82 |quote=直接民衆の語る物語からではなく)巌谷小波が定型化し、それが国定 教科書によって広く普及されたそういう桃太郎の話}}</ref> had a strong hand in establishing the forms usually known today.
===Animals in folktales=== Two creatures are particularly known for their abilities to transform into humans or other beings and objects, the ''{{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|qid=Q692111|lt=Kitsune|1=Kitsune}}'' (fox) and ''{{ill|Bake-danuki|en|Bake-danuki|qid=Q1771860|lt=tanuki|1=Bake-danuki}}'' (the {{ill|Japanese raccoon dog|en|Japanese raccoon dog|qid=Q122835073|lt=Japanese raccoon dog|1=Japanese raccoon dog}}; pictured). They occur frequently in folktales of humorous nature, such as the tanuki, Bunbuku Chagama, who could shapeshift into a teapot.
Marriages between humans and non-humans ({{Nihongo|''irui konin tan''|[[:ja:異類婚姻譚|異類婚姻譚]]|"tales of heterotype marriages"}}) comprise a major Category or motif in Japanese folklore. Japanese heterotype examples such as the crane story describes a sustained period of married life between the interspecies couple, in contrast to Western examples like [[:en:The Frog Prince (story)|Frog Prince]] or the {{ill|Leda (mythology)|en|Leda (mythology)|qid=Q182019|lt=Leda|1=Leda (mythology)}} myth where the supernatural encounter is brief. An unusual pairing occurs in the story of the {{Nihongo|''{{ill|Hamaguri nyōbo|ja|3=蛤女房|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamaguri nyōbo|1=Clam Maiden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10972198}}''|蛤女房|"clam wife"}}, which exist in both a politer written version (''[[otogi-zōshi]]'') and in a more rustic and vulgar oral tale. The gender is reversed in the tale of ''{{ill|Tanishi chōja|ja|3=田螺長者|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanishi chōja|1=Q11577267|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11577267}}'' where a bride is wedded to a tiny ''tanishi'' ([[:en:river snail|river snail]]).
===Modern renditions=== A number of folktales were adapted for stage performance by playwright {{ill|Junji Kinoshita|en|Junji Kinoshita|qid=Q552579|lt=Junji Kinoshita|1=Junji Kinoshita}}, notably ''Yūzuru'' (''[[Twilight Crane]]'', 1949),<ref>{{cite book|last=Keene|first=Donald |title=Dawn to the West: Japanese Literature of the Modern Era |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=1999 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kw7q73qBIMoC&pg=PA482 |isbn=978-0-231-dd439-4 |pages=482–483}}</ref> based on the folktale ''{{ill|Tsuru no Ongaeshi|en|Tsuru no Ongaeshi|qid=Q4121243|lt=Tsuru no Ongaeshi|1=Tsuru no Ongaeshi}}'' or "a crane who repaid its gratitude".
===Fantastic creatures=== {{About||monsters and ghostly creatures in general|Yōkai|horror or ghost stories|Kaidan (disambiguation){{!}}Kaidan}} [[File:Kuniyoshi The Ghosts.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|[[:en:Kuniyoshi Utagawa|Kuniyoshi Utagawa]], ''The Ghosts'', c. 1850]]
A great deal of interest currently gravitates towards Japanese monsters taken from traditional Japanese sources. Some of the ''yōkai'' or strange beings are the stuff of folklore, orally transmitted and propagated among the populace. But one must realize that many beings or stories about them were spun and deliberately invented by professional writers during the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] and earlier, and they are not folkloric in the strict sense.
==Folk art and craft== Some well-known craft objects such as [[netsuke]], [[:en:Raccoon dog|Raccoon dog]] earthenware ({{ill|Shigaraki ware|en|Shigaraki ware|qid=Q7496308|lt=Shigaraki ware|1=Shigaraki ware}}), may be classed as {{ill|Japanese craft|en|Japanese craft|qid=Q6158727|lt=traditional Japanese crafts|1=Japanese craft}}.
A number of articles of daily household use ({{Nihongo|''mingu''|民具}}), amassed by {{ill|Keizo Shibusawa|en|Keizo Shibusawa|qid=Q981818|lt=Keizo Shibusawa|1=Keizo Shibusawa}}, became the Attic Museum collection, now mostly housed in the {{ill|National Museum of Ethnology (Japan)|en|National Museum of Ethnology (Japan)|qid=Q147157|lt=National Museum of Ethnology|1=National Museum of Ethnology (Japan)}} in [[:en:Suita, Osaka|Suita, Osaka]]. The {{ill|Mingei|en|Mingei|qid=Q3314961|lt=Mingei|1=Mingei}} movement spearheaded by {{ill|Yanagi Sōetsu|en|Yanagi Sōetsu|qid=Q904161|lt=Yanagi Sōetsu|1=Yanagi Sōetsu}} sought to appreciate folk craft from an aesthetic viewpoint.
===Representative art=== *{{ill|Otsu-e|en|Otsu-e|qid=Q11437366|lt=Ōtsu-e|1=Otsu-e}}, a type of folk painting produced in {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|qid=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu|1=Ōtsu}} in {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}} often depicting ogre-like figures, purchased as amulet for travelers. *{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|qid=Q687288|lt=ema|1=Ema (Shinto)}}, wooden plaques with paintings of horses or other figures, on which wishes are written and hung in shrines. *{{ill|Koinobori|en|Koinobori|qid=Q779745|lt=Koinobori|1=Koinobori}}, carp-shaped banners.
===Toys=== *{{ill|zuguri|ja|3=ずぐり独楽|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zuguri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a type of {{ill|spinning top|en|spinning top|qid=Q23856|lt=top|1=Spinning top}} with concentric patterns drawn in the concave depression({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Akabeko|en|Akabeko|qid=Q1660457|lt=Akabeko|1=Akabeko}}, a red papier-mâché bull or cow with bobbing head. *{{ill|Okiagari-koboshi|en|Okiagari-koboshi|qid=Q769814|lt=Okiagari-koboshi|1=Okiagari-koboshi}}, a legless bottom-loaded doll that rights itself. *[[:en:Miharu-goma|Miharu-goma]], ({{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}), Yawatauma ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}), Kinoshita-goma ({{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|qid=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}) are the three major wooden carved horse figurines.
===Textiles=== * ''{{ill|kogin-zashi|en|kogin-zashi|qid=Q11266236|lt=kogin-zashi|1=Kogin-zashi}}'' ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}), a type of quilted clothing.
===Articles of clothing=== Some of the articles below are essential for understanding traditional Japanese culture. The type of material used is also part of folklore. * {{ill|Kasa (hat)|en|Kasa (hat)|qid=Q507408|lt=kasa|1=Kasa (hat)}} are hats woven from [[:en:sedge|sedge]], [[:en:soft rush|soft rush]], strips of {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|qid=Q670887|lt=Bamboo|1=Bamboo}}, or strips of [[hinoki]] cypress. * {{ill|Mino (straw cape)|en|Mino (straw cape)|qid=Q708965|lt=mino|1=Mino (straw cape)}}, bushy cape made from rice straw, were used as rain gear and snow gear. Snow boots were also woven from rice straw. * {{ill|Waraji|en|Waraji|qid=Q696696|lt=Waraji|1=Waraji}}, straw-woven footwear. * ''bandori'' ({{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} and other regions) is a type of often colorfully woven back strap used when carrying loads on one's back<ref>{{cite web |url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=86556 |title=庄内のばんどりコレクション |language=ja |trans-title=Shonai no Bandori Collection |website=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2012-05-03 |archive-date=2012-08-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120822102332/http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=86556 |url-status=dead }} (virtual gallery)</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Japanese urban legend|Japanese urban legend]] *{{ill|List of legendary creatures from Japan|en|List of legendary creatures from Japan|qid=Q2731245|lt=List of legendary creatures from Japan|1=List of legendary creatures from Japan}}
==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{Refbegin}} ;Dictionaries and encyclopedias * {{cite book|last=Murakami|first=Shigeyoshi|script-title=ja:日本宗教事典 |trans-title=Japanese religion dictionary |publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-4-06-158837-0}} *{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shintō|publisher=Psychology Press |year=1997|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y0TIfrZuq_YC&pg=PA98|isbn= 978-0-7007-1051-5}}
;Monograms, studies *{{cite book|last=Bernard|first=FRank |title=Kata-imi et kata-tagae; étude sur les interdits de direction á l'époque Heian|place=Tokyo|publisher=Presses Universitaires de France|year=1958}} *{{cite book|editor1-last=Bestor|editor1-first=Victoria Lyon |editor2-last=Bestor|editor2-first=Theodore C. |others=Akiko Yamagata |date=20dd |title=Routledge Handbook of Japanese Culture and Society |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-136-73627-8 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0cBYffHp5L4C&pg=PA69}} *{{cite book|last1=Bownas|first1=Geoffrey|last2=Brown |first2=Pauline|title=Japanese Rainmaking And Other Folk Practices |publisher=Psychology Press |year=2004 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uUPKlm94w5sC&pg=PA23 |isbn= 978-0-415-33069-5}}, *{{cite book|last1=Kornicki|first1=Peter Francis|last2=McMullen |first2=James|title=Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth |publisher=Cambridge University Press |year=1996 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gsLDwvmnt_oC&pg=PA87 |isbn= 978-0-521-55028-4}} *{{cite book|last=Plutschow|first=Herbert E.|title=Chaos and Cosmos: Ritual in Early and Medieval Japanese Literature |publisher=Brill |year=1990 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pwfUl-sHReYC&pg=PA60 |isbn= 978-90-04-08628-9}} *{{cite book|last=Schnelle|first=Scott|title=The Rousing Drum: Ritual Practice in a Japanese Community |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=1999 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X5phSpQr5kkC&pg=PA325 |isbn= 978-0-8248-2141-8}} {{Refend}}
== Further reading == * {{cite book |first=Hiroko |last=Ikeda |title=A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature |series=Folklore Fellows Communications |volume=184 |publisher=Suomalainen tiedeakatemia |date=1971}} * {{Cite book |first=Hiroko |last=Ikeda |chapter=Type Indexing the Folk Narrative |title=Directions in Pacific Traditional Literature. Essays in Honor of Katharine Luomala |date=1976 |editor1=Adrienne L. Kaeppler |editor2=H. Arlo Nimmo |location=Honolulu |publisher=[[Bishop Museum Press]] |pages=331–350 |isbn=0910240205}} * {{Cite book|last=Kunio|first=Yanagita|title=The Yanagita Kunio Guide to the Japanese Folk Tale|date=1986|publisher=[[Indiana University Press]]|isbn=0-253-36812-X|translator-last=Mayer|translator-first=Fanny Hagin|oclc=13124829|author-link=Kunio Yanagita}} * {{Cite journal|last=Seki|first=Keigo|author-link=Keigo Seki|date=1966|title=Types of Japanese Folktales|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/dd77478|journal=[[Asian Folklore Studies]]|volume=25|issue=1|pages=17–25|doi=10.2307/dd77478|jstor=dd77478}}
